A key example of interpretatio Romana is the god Mercury , identified as the Celtic deity Lugus , who personified kingship and was a patron of arts , crafts , travelers , and merchants . His name appears in place - names across Europe ( e . g . , Lyon , Laon , Leiden ) and survives in medieval literature as Lugh and Lleu . His festival , Lughnasa , is still celebrated in parts of Ireland , and Mercury became the most depicted deity in Roman Gaul . The goddess Minerva corresponds to the Celtic figure Brigit , known for healing , smithcraft , and poetry . Her cult was resilient , leading to her canonization as St . Brigit of Kildare , one of Ireland ' s patron saints . Caesar noted that the Gauls claimed descent from Dis Pater , the Roman god of the underworld , reflecting a belief in a divine ancestor , possibly the Dagda or Donn , suggesting Romans recognized structural similarities between the pantheons . The triad of Jupiter , Mars , and Mercury frequently appears in Gallo - Roman inscriptions , often alongside local deities . The Pillar of the Boatmen ( Pilier des Nautes ) in Paris exemplifies this syncretism , depicting both Roman and Gaulish gods . Taranis , meaning " thunder , " was identified with Jupiter , with archaeological evidence from Raetia showing this connection in sculptures , indicating local acceptance . Apollo was associated with several Celtic deities , notably Grannus and Borvo . Apollo Grannus had a major sanctuary at Phoebiana ( modern Lauingen , Germany ) , often paired with Sirona , a Celtic goddess of healing springs , a common pairing in eastern Gaul and the Rhineland . сократить текст на английском
